title: Building the React Flow Bundle
text: Node.js (v18 higher): Download nodejs.org npm (comes Node.js)

what: Option 1: Using R (from a source checkout)
title: Building the React Flow Bundle
text: working directory root dplyneage repository:
code: # Check if bundle already exists dplyneage::has_bundle()  # Build (or force-rebuild) the bundle - internal developer helper dplyneage:::build_bundle() dplyneage:::build_bundle(force = TRUE)

title: Building the React Flow Bundle
text: package source directory terminal:
code: chmod +x build_bundle.sh ./build_bundle.sh

title: Building the React Flow Bundle
code: cd srcjs npm install npm run build

title: Building the React Flow Bundle
text: build process creates: inst/htmlwidgets/lib/reactflow/reactflow-bundle.min.js - Complete React Flow bundle bundle includes: React 18.2.0 ReactDOM 18.2.0 @xyflow/react (React Flow) 12.10.0 necessary CSS (injected automatically)

title: Building the React Flow Bundle
text: active development auto-rebuild: watch changes rebuild automatically.
code: cd srcjs npm run dev

title: Building the React Flow Bundle
text: bundle loads successfully, ’ll see interactive React Flow diagram draggable nodes, pan/zoom controls, grid background. bundle doesn’t load, fallback SVG visualization.
code: devtools::load_all()  lineage_flow(   nodes = list(     list(id = "1", position = list(x = 0, y = 0),           data = list(label = "Source")),     list(id = "2", position = list(x = 250, y = 100),           data = list(label = "Transform")),     list(id = "3", position = list(x = 500, y = 0),           data = list(label = "Output"))   ),   edges = list(     list(id = "e1-2", source = "1", target = "2"),     list(id = "e2-3", source = "2", target = "3")   ) )

title: Building the React Flow Bundle
text: Bundle found: Make sure build completed successfully check inst/htmlwidgets/lib/reactflow/reactflow-bundle.min.js exists. Build fails: Check Node.js version: node --version (v18+) Clear node_modules rebuild: cd srcjs && rm -rf node_modules && npm install && npm run build Widget shows SVG instead React Flow: Check browser console errors. widget fallback SVG React Flow fails load.

title: Building the React Flow Bundle
text: ’re modifying React Flow bundle: Make changes srcjs/src/index.js Run npm run dev srcjs directory Reload R package devtools::load_all() Test changes webpack configuration srcjs/webpack.config.js handles bundling dependencies single minified file.

title: Building the React Flow Bundle
text: bundle created using webpack includes: React Flow: main visualization library React & ReactDOM: Required React Flow CSS: styling bundled inline Source maps: debugging (development mode) bundle optimized production minification tree-shaking reduce file size.

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: dplyr/dbplyr pipelines analyzed entirely R, nothing else install. Raw SQL strings analyzed sqlglot, dplyneage’s one Python dependency — declared via reticulate::py_require() provisioned automatically first time ’s needed, setup step. See vignette("python-integration") manage Python environment.
code: pak::pak("tgerke/dplyneage")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: Let’s create small -memory DuckDB database work : simplest lineage — two columns selected one table: output column connects back source column came . Try dragging tables around, zooming mouse wheel, hovering column highlight connections.
code: library(dplyneage) library(dplyr) library(duckdb)  con <- dbConnect(duckdb(), ":memory:")  customers <- tibble(   id = 1:5,   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana", "Eve"),   email = paste0(tolower(name), "@example.com") )  orders <- tibble(   order_id = 1:10,   customer_id = rep(1:5, each = 2),   amount = c(100, 150, 200, 75, 300, 125, 180, 90, 250, 160) )  copy_to(con, customers, "customers", overwrite = TRUE) copy_to(con, orders, "orders", overwrite = TRUE) tbl(con, "customers") |>   select(id, name) |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: Lineage becomes useful transformations pile . join followed aggregation: Notice total_spent traces back orders.amount — customers, even though amount appears unqualified generated SQL. pass dbplyr table, dplyneage doesn’t parse SQL : walks pipeline’s query tree, records exactly table column came , attribution always right. pipeline embeds raw SQL dbplyr::sql(), query tree can’t see inside string, extract_lineage() hands whole query sqlglot instead (message). can also force specific engine engine = "r" engine = "sqlglot" — see ?extract_lineage.
code: tbl(con, "customers") |>   left_join(tbl(con, "orders"), by = c("id" = "customer_id")) |>   group_by(id, name) |>   summarise(total_spent = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow(height = "400px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: cases break naive lineage tools. dplyneage handles engines resolve full query structure rather pattern-matching column names: dbplyr pipelines query tree, raw SQL sqlglot’s lineage engine. ’ll use raw SQL keep examples compact.

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: Columns traced intermediate CTEs back base tables — recent transparent, amount correctly attributes orders:
code: extract_lineage("   WITH recent AS (     SELECT customer_id, amount FROM orders WHERE order_date > '2024-01-01'   )   SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM recent GROUP BY customer_id ") |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: computed column can come several tables . COALESCE full join gets edge sources: applies arithmetic across tables (o.amount * r.rate), CASE expressions, branches UNION. dbplyr pipelines get treatment: full_join() key column traces sides, union_all() column every branch.
code: extract_lineage("   SELECT COALESCE(u.email, a.email) AS email   FROM users u FULL JOIN archive a ON u.id = a.id ") |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: schema available, SELECT * expands real columns. dbplyr input never needs schema — pipeline knows columns — raw SQL, pass one :
code: extract_lineage(   "SELECT * FROM customers",   schema = list(customers = c("id", "name", "email")) ) |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: examples show, extract_lineage() accepts SQL string directly — useful auditing queries didn’t write R. Two things know: Qualified columns (o.amount) always resolve correctly, schema . Unqualified columns need schema attributed certainty. Pass named list mapping table columns: Without schema, SELECT * expanded produces warning rather silently empty diagram. Queries SQL dialects work setting dialect:
code: extract_lineage(   "SELECT c.name, order_date    FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id",   schema = list(     customers = c("id", "name", "email"),     orders = c("order_id", "customer_id", "order_date", "amount")   ) ) |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px") extract_lineage(query, dialect = "postgres") extract_lineage(query, dialect = "snowflake")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: documentation design sketches, skip extraction entirely build diagram : Nodes come three types — "source" (blue), "transform" (orange), "target" (green) — edges accept label (e.g. "SUM()") animated = TRUE emphasis. lineage_example() renders complete hand-built diagram can use template.
code: nodes <- list(   create_table_node(     table_name = "customers",     columns = c("id", "name", "email"),     x = 0, y = 100,     table_type = "source"   ),   create_table_node(     table_name = "customer_summary",     columns = c("customer_id", "full_name", "contact"),     x = 500, y = 100,     table_type = "target"   ) )  edges <- list(   create_column_edge("customers", "id", "customer_summary", "customer_id"),   create_column_edge("customers", "name", "customer_summary", "full_name"),   create_column_edge("customers", "email", "customer_summary", "contact") )  lineage_flow(nodes, edges, height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: Diagrams answer questions interactively; sometimes need lineage plain data. Two exporters cover common cases. lineage_json() serializes nodes, edges, metadata small, stable JSON document. output deterministic, can commit alongside pipeline code let CI diff : refactor silently changes column comes , diff shows ships. also natural handoff format data catalogs anything scriptable jq. lineage_graphml() writes GraphML, XML format graph tools speak: igraph, Gephi, yEd open directly. Every column becomes node, makes real graph queries possible. classic one impact analysis — “orders.amount changes, outputs affected?” — reverse, tracing output back every source column feeds : functions return serialized string called without path, compose pipes tests.
code:
lineage <- tbl(con, "customers") |>   left_join(tbl(con, "orders"), by = c("id" = "customer_id")) |>   group_by(id, name) |>   summarise(total_spent = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>   extract_lineage()  lineage_json(lineage) #> { #>   "metadata": { #>     "sql": "SELECT id, "name", SUM(amount) AS total_spent
FROM (
  SELECT customers.*, order_id, amount
  FROM customers
  LEFT JOIN orders
    ON (customers.id = orders.customer_id)
) AS q01
GROUP BY id, "name"", #>     "dialect": "duckdb", #>     "engine": "r", #>     "table_count": 3, #>     "edge_count": 3 #>   }, #>   "nodes": [ #>     { #>       "id": "customers", #>       "type": "source", #>       "columns": ["id", "name"] #>     }, #>     { #>       "id": "orders", #>       "type": "source", #>       "columns": ["amount"] #>     }, #>     { #>       "id": "output", #>       "type": "target", #>       "columns": ["id", "name", "total_spent"] #>     } #>   ], #>   "edges": [ #>     { #>       "source": "customers", #>       "source_column": "id", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "id" #>     }, #>     { #>       "source": "customers", #>       "source_column": "name", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "name" #>     }, #>     { #>       "source": "orders", #>       "source_column": "amount", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "total_spent" #>     } #>   ] #> } path <- tempfile(fileext = ".graphml") lineage_graphml(lineage, path)  g <- igraph::read_graph(path, format = "graphml")  # Everything upstream of total_spent igraph::subcomponent(g, "output.total_spent", mode = "in") #> + 2/6 vertices, named, from 9c01206: #> [1] output.total_spent orders.amount  # Everything downstream of orders.amount igraph::subcomponent(g, "orders.amount", mode = "out") #> + 2/6 vertices, named, from 9c01206: #> [1] orders.amount      output.total_spent

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: vignette("python-integration") explains Python dependency managed, use environment function reference documents every argument Found query traces incorrectly? Please open issue SQL

what: Installation: There Is No Step Two
title: Python Integration
text: Python setup automatic. first time lineage extraction needs sqlglot, reticulate : Find suitable Python (downloading self-contained build via uv none configured) Provision ephemeral virtual environment containing sqlglot Cache everything, subsequent sessions start quickly can verify availability time: Note: install_sqlglot() earlier development versions deprecated nothing — manual installation step anymore.
code: library(dplyneage)  # This just works - no install step required extract_lineage("SELECT id, name FROM customers") |>   lineage_flow() has_sqlglot() #> [1] TRUE

title: Python Integration
text: manage Python environment (project virtualenv, conda env, system Python), reticulate respect usual. Just make sure sqlglot installed : See ?reticulate::use_virtualenv reticulate Python version docs ways select environment.
code: pip install 'sqlglot>=23.0.0' # Point reticulate at your environment before loading dplyneage Sys.setenv(RETICULATE_PYTHON = "/path/to/your/python")  library(dplyneage) has_sqlglot()

title: Python Integration
text: engines emit lineage metadata, everything downstream shared. one runs controlled engine argument extract_lineage(): "auto" (default) picks R engine lazy tables sqlglot SQL strings, falling back sqlglot pipeline uses something R engine trace. metadata$engine result records one ran.
code: dbplyr pipeline ──→ pure-R walk of the lazy query tree                                   │ raw SQL string ──→ Python (sqlglot.lineage engine)                                   ↓               Column Lineage Metadata (per output column)                                   ↓                     R (create nodes & edges)                                   ↓                  React Flow Visualization

title: Python Integration
text: Walks dbplyr’s lazy query tree, reading exact column provenance selects, mutates, joins, set operations — SQL parsing, Python .onLoad(): declares sqlglot requirement via py_require() imports bundled Python module delay_load (Python start first use) has_sqlglot(): checks availability Built sqlglot.lineage.lineage(), handles scope resolution, aliases, CTE trace-, set operations, star expansion extract_lineage(): traces output column source columns list_tables(): enumerates base tables (used schema harvesting) extract_lineage(): main user-facing function, dispatches engine harvest_schema(): reads table schemas database connection unqualified columns attributed right table convert_lineage_to_graph(): creates visualization nodes edges

title: Python Integration
text: SQL alone always say table unqualified column belongs . dbplyr lazy tables sidestep problem entirely: R engine reads provenance query tree, schema ever needed. (lazy table falls back sqlglot, dplyneage lists columns referenced table live connection hands schema sqlglot automatically.) raw SQL strings, can pass schema : Without schema, fully qualified columns still resolve correctly; unqualified ones may traceable, SELECT * expanded (’ll get warning).
code: extract_lineage(   "SELECT c.name, order_date FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id",   schema = list(     customers = c("id", "name"),     orders = c("customer_id", "order_date")   ) )

title: Python Integration
text: sqlglot supports many SQL dialects. Specify dialect extracting lineage: dialect match database backend ensure accurate parsing. See sqlglot documentation full list dialects.
code: extract_lineage(query, dialect = "duckdb")     # default extract_lineage(query, dialect = "postgres") extract_lineage(query, dialect = "snowflake") extract_lineage(query, dialect = "bigquery") extract_lineage(query, dialect = "mysql")

title: Python Integration
text: dbplyr pipelines: Python startup cost — R engine runs immediately First raw-SQL call: may take moment Python environment initializes (, first run, provisions) Subsequent calls: fast (<100ms typical queries) Complex queries: sqlglot handles CTEs, subqueries, window functions, complex joins, set operations (UNION, INTERSECT, etc.)

title: Python Integration
code: # See which Python reticulate is using reticulate::py_config()  # List installed packages reticulate::py_list_packages()

what: sqlglot not found in a custom environment
title: Python Integration
text: has_sqlglot() returns FALSE set RETICULATE_PYTHON (activated environment), sqlglot missing environment — install pip install sqlglot. custom configuration, reticulate provision automatically; see ?reticulate::py_require details.

title: Python Integration
text: Reticulate Package Documentation Embedding Python R Packages sqlglot Documentation sqlglot GitHub

what: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: dplyneage draws interactive column-level lineage diagrams dplyr dbplyr pipelines. Pipe query extract_lineage() traces every output column back source columns came — joins, aggregations, CTEs, unions, computed expressions — renders result draggable, zoomable React Flow diagram lineage_flow(). dbplyr pipelines analyzed pure R walking lazy query tree, Python involved. Raw SQL goes sqlglot’s dedicated lineage engine instead, means many dialects (DuckDB, PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, …) work .

title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: dbplyr pipelines need Python . raw SQL input, Python dependency (sqlglot) provisioned automatically first time ’s needed, via reticulate::py_require() — setup step. See vignette("python-integration") manage Python environment.
code: pak::pak("tgerke/dplyneage")

title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: Build dplyr pipeline database usual, pipe extract_lineage() lineage_flow():  Behind one pipe, extract_lineage(): walks pipeline’s lazy query tree pure R, tracing every output column source columns (joins, aggregations, unions, multi-source computed columns resolve exactly) falls back sqlglot’s lineage engine pipeline injects raw SQL dbplyr::sql(), pass SQL string directly (path handles aliases, CTEs, subqueries, reads table schemas connection unqualified columns attribute correctly) resulting diagram fully interactive: drag tables rearrange, zoom pan, hover columns highlight connections.
code: library(dplyneage) library(dplyr) library(dbplyr) library(duckdb)  con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), ":memory:")  customers <- tibble(   customer_id = 1:5,   first_name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana", "Eve"),   last_name = c("Smith", "Jones", "Brown", "Wilson", "Davis"),   email = paste0(tolower(first_name), "@example.com") )  orders <- tibble(   order_id = 1:10,   customer_id = rep(1:5, each = 2),   amount = c(100, 150, 200, 75, 300, 125, 180, 90, 250, 160) )  copy_to(con, customers, "customers", overwrite = TRUE) copy_to(con, orders, "orders", overwrite = TRUE)  tbl(con, "customers") |>   select(customer_id, first_name, last_name, email) |>   left_join(tbl(con, "orders"), by = "customer_id") |>   group_by(customer_id, first_name, last_name, email) |>   summarise(     total_orders = n(),     total_spent = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   ) |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow(height = "600px")

title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: documentation design work, can construct lineage diagrams directly create_table_node() create_column_edge():  Table types follow color conventions used dbt SQLMesh:
code: nodes <- list(   create_table_node(     table_name = "customers",     columns = c("customer_id", "name", "email"),     x = 0, y = 50,     table_type = "source"   ),   create_table_node(     table_name = "orders",     columns = c("order_id", "customer_id", "total_amount"),     x = 0, y = 300,     table_type = "source"   ),   create_table_node(     table_name = "customer_summary",     columns = c("customer_id", "customer_name", "total_spent"),     x = 500, y = 150,     table_type = "target"   ) )  edges <- list(   create_column_edge("customers", "customer_id", "customer_summary", "customer_id"),   create_column_edge("customers", "name", "customer_summary", "customer_name"),   create_column_edge("orders", "total_amount", "customer_summary", "total_spent",                      label = "SUM()", animated = TRUE) )  lineage_flow(nodes, edges, height = "600px") #> file:////private/var/folders/fw/0d9nr9951q57f0d5l6qc1j200000gn/T/RtmpRr8BR4/file36121c0b9ae9/widget36123de74561.html screenshot completed

title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: extract_lineage() accepts dbplyr lazy table, composes directly packages produce — example ducklake tables:
code: library(ducklake)  get_ducklake_table("orders") |>   dplyr::left_join(get_ducklake_table("customers"), by = "customer_id") |>   dplyr::group_by(customer_id) |>   dplyr::summarise(total = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE)) |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow()

title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: Diagrams people; lineage also useful plain data. lineage_json() gives small, stable document can query jq, feed data catalog, commit next pipeline code — CI diff flags change column provenance ships: Written file, document scriptable outside R entirely — ’s jq answering “source columns feed total_spent?”: lineage_graphml() writes GraphML, opens directly graph tools like Gephi, yEd, igraph. question works graph query — scales transitive ancestry pipelines chain:
code:
lineage <- tbl(con, "orders") |>   left_join(tbl(con, "customers"), by = "customer_id") |>   group_by(customer_id, first_name) |>   summarise(total_spent = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>   extract_lineage()  lineage_json(lineage) #> { #>   "metadata": { #>     "sql": "SELECT customer_id, first_name, SUM(amount) AS total_spent
FROM (
  SELECT orders.*, first_name, last_name, email
  FROM orders
  LEFT JOIN customers
    ON (orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id)
) AS q01
GROUP BY customer_id, first_name", #>     "dialect": "duckdb", #>     "engine": "r", #>     "table_count": 3, #>     "edge_count": 3 #>   }, #>   "nodes": [ #>     { #>       "id": "orders", #>       "type": "source", #>       "columns": ["customer_id", "amount"] #>     }, #>     { #>       "id": "customers", #>       "type": "source", #>       "columns": ["first_name"] #>     }, #>     { #>       "id": "output", #>       "type": "target", #>       "columns": ["customer_id", "first_name", "total_spent"] #>     } #>   ], #>   "edges": [ #>     { #>       "source": "orders", #>       "source_column": "customer_id", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "customer_id" #>     }, #>     { #>       "source": "customers", #>       "source_column": "first_name", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "first_name" #>     }, #>     { #>       "source": "orders", #>       "source_column": "amount", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "total_spent" #>     } #>   ] #> } lineage_json(lineage, "lineage.json") jq -r '.edges[] | select(.target_column == "total_spent")        | "\(.source).\(.source_column)"' lineage.json #> orders.amount path <- tempfile(fileext = ".graphml") lineage_graphml(lineage, path)  g <- igraph::read_graph(path, format = "graphml") igraph::subcomponent(g, "output.total_spent", mode = "in") #> + 2/6 vertices, named, from 2e62c52: #> [1] output.total_spent orders.amount

title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: vignette("getting-started") walks first diagram CTEs, multi-source columns, schemas vignette("python-integration") covers Python dependency managed Full function reference tgerke.github.io/dplyneage

title: Build React Flow Bundle — build_bundle
text: Developer tool rebuilds React Flow JavaScript bundle srcjs/ sources. bundle ships pre-built package, end users never need ; works source checkout repository (see also build_bundle.sh).

title: Build React Flow Bundle — build_bundle
code: build_bundle(force = FALSE)

title: Build React Flow Bundle — build_bundle
text: force TRUE, rebuilds even bundle already exists. Default: FALSE

title: Build React Flow Bundle — build_bundle
text: Invisibly returns TRUE successful, FALSE failed

title: Build React Flow Bundle — build_bundle
text: Requires Node.js (v18+) npm.

what: Convert Lineage Data to Graph Structure — convert_lineage_to_graph
title: Convert Lineage Data to Graph Structure — convert_lineage_to_graph
text: Converts lineage information nodes edges visualization

title: Convert Lineage Data to Graph Structure — convert_lineage_to_graph
code: convert_lineage_to_graph(lineage_data)

title: Convert Lineage Data to Graph Structure — convert_lineage_to_graph
text: lineage_data Processed lineage data

title: Convert Lineage Data to Graph Structure — convert_lineage_to_graph
text: List nodes edges

what: Connect two columns in a lineage diagram — create_column_edge
title: Connect two columns in a lineage diagram — create_column_edge
text: Creates edge one table's column another's, diagrams built create_table_node() rendered lineage_flow(). Table column names must match table_name columns used creating nodes.

title: Connect two columns in a lineage diagram — create_column_edge
code: create_column_edge(   from_table,   from_column,   to_table,   to_column,   label = NULL,   animated = FALSE )

title: Connect two columns in a lineage diagram — create_column_edge
text: from_table, from_column Table column data comes . to_table, to_column Table column data flows . label Optional label drawn edge, typically transformation applied (e.g. "SUM()"). animated TRUE, edge drawn moving dash pattern. Useful drawing attention aggregations.

title: Connect two columns in a lineage diagram — create_column_edge
text: edge list ready pass lineage_flow()

title: Connect two columns in a lineage diagram — create_column_edge
code: # A direct column mapping create_column_edge("customers", "id", "customer_summary", "customer_id") #> $id #> [1] "e_customers.id_to_customer_summary.customer_id" #>  #> $source #> [1] "customers" #>  #> $target #> [1] "customer_summary" #>  #> $sourceHandle #> [1] "id" #>  #> $targetHandle #> [1] "customer_id" #>  #> $animated #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $style #> $style$stroke #> [1] "#64748b" #>  #> $style$strokeWidth #> [1] 2 #>  #>   # An aggregation, labeled and animated create_column_edge("orders", "amount", "customer_summary", "total_spent",   label = "SUM()", animated = TRUE ) #> $id #> [1] "e_orders.amount_to_customer_summary.total_spent" #>  #> $source #> [1] "orders" #>  #> $target #> [1] "customer_summary" #>  #> $sourceHandle #> [1] "amount" #>  #> $targetHandle #> [1] "total_spent" #>  #> $animated #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $style #> $style$stroke #> [1] "#64748b" #>  #> $style$strokeWidth #> [1] 2 #>  #>  #> $label #> [1] "SUM()" #>  #> $labelStyle #> $labelStyle$fill #> [1] "#64748b" #>  #> $labelStyle$fontWeight #> [1] 500 #>  #> $labelStyle$fontSize #> [1] 11 #>  #>  #> $labelBgStyle #> $labelBgStyle$fill #> [1] "#ffffff" #>  #> $labelBgStyle$fillOpacity #> [1] 0.9 #>  #>

what: Create a table node for a lineage diagram — create_table_node
title: Create a table node for a lineage diagram — create_table_node
text: Builds one table, columns, lineage diagrams rendered lineage_flow(). Use together create_column_edge() want full control diagram instead extracting lineage automatically.

title: Create a table node for a lineage diagram — create_table_node
code: create_table_node(table_name, columns, x = 0, y = 0, table_type = "source")

title: Create a table node for a lineage diagram — create_table_node
text: table_name Name shown node header. Also used node id, must unique within diagram. columns Character vector column names listed node. column gets connection handles edges can attach . x, y Position node canvas, pixels. Nodes remain draggable, set starting layout. table_type One "source" (blue), "transform" (orange), "target" (green). colors follow conventions used tools like dbt SQLMesh.

title: Create a table node for a lineage diagram — create_table_node
text: node list ready pass lineage_flow()

title: Create a table node for a lineage diagram — create_table_node
code: create_table_node(   table_name = "customers",   columns = c("id", "name", "email"),   table_type = "source" ) #> $id #> [1] "customers" #>  #> $type #> [1] "tableNode" #>  #> $data #> $data$label #> [1] "customers" #>  #> $data$columns #> [1] "id"    "name"  "email" #>  #> $data$tableType #> [1] "source" #>  #> $data$colors #> $data$colors$bg #> [1] "#f0f7ff" #>  #> $data$colors$border #> [1] "#3b82f6" #>  #> $data$colors$header #> [1] "#1d4ed8" #>  #>  #>  #> $position #> $position$x #> [1] 0 #>  #> $position$y #> [1] 0 #>  #>  #> $draggable #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $sourcePosition #> [1] "right" #>  #> $targetPosition #> [1] "left" #>

what: dplyneage: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines — dplyneage-package
title: dplyneage: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines — dplyneage-package
text: Implements column lineage visualizations using React Flow dplyr dbplyr pipelines. Provides tidyverse-style interface tracking data transformations pipeline operations.

title: dplyneage: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines — dplyneage-package
text: Maintainer: Travis Gerke tgerke@mail.harvard.edu Authors: Travis Gerke tgerke@mail.harvard.edu

what: Extract column lineage from a dplyr pipeline or SQL query — extract_lineage
title: Extract column lineage from a dplyr pipeline or SQL query — extract_lineage
text: extract_lineage() traces every output column query back source table columns computed . Pipe dbplyr lazy table straight , pass SQL string. Aliases, CTEs, subqueries, set operations like UNION, multi-source expressions COALESCE(.x, b.x) resolve true source columns.

title: Extract column lineage from a dplyr pipeline or SQL query — extract_lineage
code: extract_lineage(   sql,   dialect = "duckdb",   schema = NULL,   show_sql = FALSE,   engine = c("auto", "sqlglot", "r") )

title: Extract column lineage from a dplyr pipeline or SQL query — extract_lineage
text: sql dbplyr lazy table (tbl_lazy) single SQL query string. Lazy tables analyzed directly lazy query tree (SQL recorded metadata still comes dbplyr::sql_render()); one handled sqlglot engine instead, database connection used harvest table schemas automatically. dialect SQL dialect query written , e.g. "duckdb" (default), "postgres", "mysql", "snowflake", "bigquery". dialect sqlglot understands works . schema Optional table schema used sqlglot engine attribute unqualified columns right table expand SELECT *: named list mapping table names character vectors column names, e.g. list(orders = c("order_id", "amount")). relevant SQL strings — R engine reads exact provenance lazy query tree, lazy table falls back sqlglot harvests schema database connection automatically. show_sql TRUE, print SQL analyzed. Useful seeing dbplyr generated pipeline. Default: FALSE. engine lineage engine use. "auto" (default) uses pure-R engine lazy tables dbplyr (>= 2.5.0) installed, falling back sqlglot SQL strings unsupported constructs. "r" forces pure-R engine errors anything trace. "sqlglot" always renders SQL analyzes sqlglot.

title: Extract column lineage from a dplyr pipeline or SQL query — extract_lineage
text: list nodes edges ready pass lineage_flow(), plus metadata recording analyzed SQL, dialect, engine used, node/edge counts.

title: Extract column lineage from a dplyr pipeline or SQL query — extract_lineage
text: Two engines available. dbplyr lazy tables analyzed pure-R fast path walks pipeline's lazy query tree directly — Python required. SQL strings analyzed sqlglot's lineage engine via reticulate. pipeline uses construct R engine trace (e.g. raw SQL injected dbplyr::sql()), falls back sqlglot automatically. engines trace select-list lineage: columns used filter(), join conditions, arrange() create lineage edges.

title: Extract column lineage from a dplyr pipeline or SQL query — extract_lineage
code: # Raw SQL: qualified columns resolve on their own extract_lineage("SELECT c.id, c.name FROM customers c") |>   lineage_flow()  {"x":{"nodes":[{"id":"customers","type":"tableNode","data":{"label":"customers","columns":["id","name"],"tableType":"source","colors":{"bg":"#f0f7ff","border":"#3b82f6","header":"#1d4ed8"}},"position":{"x":0,"y":0},"draggable":true,"sourcePosition":"right","targetPosition":"left"},{"id":"output","type":"tableNode","data":{"label":"output","columns":["id","name"],"tableType":"target","colors":{"bg":"#f0fdf4","border":"#10b981","header":"#059669"}},"position":{"x":400,"y":0},"draggable":true,"sourcePosition":"right","targetPosition":"left"}],"edges":[{"id":"e_customers.id_to_output.id","source":"customers","target":"output","sourceHandle":"id","targetHandle":"id","animated":false,"style":{"stroke":"#64748b","strokeWidth":2}},{"id":"e_customers.name_to_output.name","source":"customers","target":"output","sourceHandle":"name","targetHandle":"name","animated":false,"style":{"stroke":"#64748b","strokeWidth":2}}]},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]} # Supply a schema so unqualified columns attribute to the right table # and SELECT * expands extract_lineage(   "SELECT c.name, order_date FROM customers c    JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id",   schema = list(     customers = c("id", "name"),     orders = c("customer_id", "order_date")   ) ) #> $nodes #> $nodes[[1]] #> $nodes[[1]]$id #> [1] "customers" #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$type #> [1] "tableNode" #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$data #> $nodes[[1]]$data$label #> [1] "customers" #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$data$columns #> [1] "name" #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$data$tableType #> [1] "source" #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$data$colors #> $nodes[[1]]$data$colors$bg #> [1] "#f0f7ff" #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$data$colors$border #> [1] "#3b82f6" #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$data$colors$header #> [1] "#1d4ed8" #>  #>  #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$position #> $nodes[[1]]$position$x #> [1] 0 #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$position$y #> [1] 0 #>  #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$draggable #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$sourcePosition #> [1] "right" #>  #> $nodes[[1]]$targetPosition #> [1] "left" #>  #>  #> $nodes[[2]] #> $nodes[[2]]$id #> [1] "orders" #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$type #> [1] "tableNode" #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$data #> $nodes[[2]]$data$label #> [1] "orders" #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$data$columns #> [1] "order_date" #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$data$tableType #> [1] "source" #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$data$colors #> $nodes[[2]]$data$colors$bg #> [1] "#f0f7ff" #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$data$colors$border #> [1] "#3b82f6" #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$data$colors$header #> [1] "#1d4ed8" #>  #>  #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$position #> $nodes[[2]]$position$x #> [1] 0 #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$position$y #> [1] 200 #>  #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$draggable #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$sourcePosition #> [1] "right" #>  #> $nodes[[2]]$targetPosition #> [1] "left" #>  #>  #> $nodes[[3]] #> $nodes[[3]]$id #> [1] "output" #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$type #> [1] "tableNode" #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$data #> $nodes[[3]]$data$label #> [1] "output" #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$data$columns #> [1] "name"       "order_date" #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$data$tableType #> [1] "target" #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$data$colors #> $nodes[[3]]$data$colors$bg #> [1] "#f0fdf4" #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$data$colors$border #> [1] "#10b981" #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$data$colors$header #> [1] "#059669" #>  #>  #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$position #> $nodes[[3]]$position$x #> [1] 400 #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$position$y #> [1] 100 #>  #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$draggable #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$sourcePosition #> [1] "right" #>  #> $nodes[[3]]$targetPosition #> [1] "left" #>  #>  #>  #> $edges #> $edges[[1]] #> $edges[[1]]$id #> [1] "e_customers.name_to_output.name" #>  #> $edges[[1]]$source #> [1] "customers" #>  #> $edges[[1]]$target #> [1] "output" #>  #> $edges[[1]]$sourceHandle #> [1] "name" #>  #> $edges[[1]]$targetHandle #> [1] "name" #>  #> $edges[[1]]$animated #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $edges[[1]]$style #> $edges[[1]]$style$stroke #> [1] "#64748b" #>  #> $edges[[1]]$style$strokeWidth #> [1] 2 #>  #>  #>  #> $edges[[2]] #> $edges[[2]]$id #> [1] "e_orders.order_date_to_output.order_date" #>  #> $edges[[2]]$source #> [1] "orders" #>  #> $edges[[2]]$target #> [1] "output" #>  #> $edges[[2]]$sourceHandle #> [1] "order_date" #>  #> $edges[[2]]$targetHandle #> [1] "order_date" #>  #> $edges[[2]]$animated #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $edges[[2]]$style #> $edges[[2]]$style$stroke #> [1] "#64748b" #>  #> $edges[[2]]$style$strokeWidth #> [1] 2 #>  #>  #>  #>  #> $metadata #> $metadata$sql #> [1] "SELECT c.name, order_date FROM customers c\n   JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id" #>  #> $metadata$dialect #> [1] "duckdb" #>  #> $metadata$engine #> [1] "sqlglot" #>  #> $metadata$table_count #> [1] 3 #>  #> $metadata$edge_count #> [1] 2 #>  #>  # dbplyr pipelines: pipe straight in; the pure-R engine reads exact # provenance from the pipeline itself, no Python needed library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  con <- DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb()) DBI::dbWriteTable(con, "customers", data.frame(id = 1, name = "a")) DBI::dbWriteTable(con, "orders", data.frame(customer_id = 1, amount = 10))  tbl(con, "customers") |>   left_join(tbl(con, "orders"), by = c("id" = "customer_id")) |>   group_by(id, name) |>   summarise(total_spent = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow()  {"x":{"nodes":[{"id":"customers","type":"tableNode","data":{"label":"customers","columns":["id","name"],"tableType":"source","colors":{"bg":"#f0f7ff","border":"#3b82f6","header":"#1d4ed8"}},"position":{"x":0,"y":0},"draggable":true,"sourcePosition":"right","targetPosition":"left"},{"id":"orders","type":"tableNode","data":{"label":"orders","columns":"amount","tableType":"source","colors":{"bg":"#f0f7ff","border":"#3b82f6","header":"#1d4ed8"}},"position":{"x":0,"y":200},"draggable":true,"sourcePosition":"right","targetPosition":"left"},{"id":"output","type":"tableNode","data":{"label":"output","columns":["id","name","total_spent"],"tableType":"target","colors":{"bg":"#f0fdf4","border":"#10b981","header":"#059669"}},"position":{"x":400,"y":100},"draggable":true,"sourcePosition":"right","targetPosition":"left"}],"edges":[{"id":"e_customers.id_to_output.id","source":"customers","target":"output","sourceHandle":"id","targetHandle":"id","animated":false,"style":{"stroke":"#64748b","strokeWidth":2}},{"id":"e_customers.name_to_output.name","source":"customers","target":"output","sourceHandle":"name","targetHandle":"name","animated":false,"style":{"stroke":"#64748b","strokeWidth":2}},{"id":"e_orders.amount_to_output.total_spent","source":"orders","target":"output","sourceHandle":"amount","targetHandle":"total_spent","animated":false,"style":{"stroke":"#64748b","strokeWidth":2}}]},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]} DBI::dbDisconnect(con)

what: Is the Python sqlglot dependency available? — has_sqlglot
title: Is the Python sqlglot dependency available? — has_sqlglot
text: Python involved extract_lineage() analyzes raw SQL strings (falls back sqlglot pipeline trace R); dbplyr pipelines analyzed pure-R engine. dplyneage declares sqlglot dependency via reticulate::py_require(), provisioned automatically first time needed — need install anything. Use check availability, gate code extracts lineage raw SQL (examples, vignette chunks, Shiny apps). Note calling may initialize Python.

title: Is the Python sqlglot dependency available? — has_sqlglot
code: has_sqlglot()

title: Is the Python sqlglot dependency available? — has_sqlglot
text: TRUE sqlglot can loaded, FALSE otherwise

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ has_sqlglot() } # }

title: Shiny bindings for lineage_flow — lineage_flow-shiny
code: if (interactive() && requireNamespace("shiny", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(shiny)    ui <- fluidPage(     lineage_flowOutput("lineage", height = "600px")   )   server <- function(input, output, session) {     output$lineage <- renderLineageFlow(lineage_example())   }   shinyApp(ui, server) }

what: Render an interactive column lineage diagram — lineage_flow
title: Render an interactive column lineage diagram — lineage_flow
text: Draws lineage graph React Flow: tables draggable nodes, column--column edges, zoom/pan controls. Pass result extract_lineage() directly (detected automatically, piping works), build nodes edges create_table_node() create_column_edge().

title: Render an interactive column lineage diagram — lineage_flow
code: lineage_flow(   nodes = list(),   edges = list(),   width = NULL,   height = NULL,   elementId = NULL )

title: Render an interactive column lineage diagram — lineage_flow
text: nodes output extract_lineage(), list nodes created create_table_node(). edges list edges created create_column_edge(). Ignored nodes extract_lineage() result, carries edges. width, height CSS dimensions widget, e.g. "100%" "600px". Default full width 600px tall. elementId Explicit HTML element id widget. Usually left NULL one generated.

title: Render an interactive column lineage diagram — lineage_flow
text: htmlwidget prints RStudio viewer, R Markdown / Quarto documents, Shiny apps.

title: Render an interactive column lineage diagram — lineage_flow
code: # Build a small diagram by hand nodes <- list(   create_table_node("orders", c("order_id", "amount"), x = 0, y = 0),   create_table_node("daily_totals", c("total"),     x = 400, y = 0, table_type = "target"   ) ) edges <- list(   create_column_edge("orders", "amount", "daily_totals", "total",     label = "SUM()", animated = TRUE   ) ) lineage_flow(nodes, edges)  {"x":{"nodes":[{"id":"orders","type":"tableNode","data":{"label":"orders","columns":["order_id","amount"],"tableType":"source","colors":{"bg":"#f0f7ff","border":"#3b82f6","header":"#1d4ed8"}},"position":{"x":0,"y":0},"draggable":true,"sourcePosition":"right","targetPosition":"left"},{"id":"daily_totals","type":"tableNode","data":{"label":"daily_totals","columns":"total","tableType":"target","colors":{"bg":"#f0fdf4","border":"#10b981","header":"#059669"}},"position":{"x":400,"y":0},"draggable":true,"sourcePosition":"right","targetPosition":"left"}],"edges":[{"id":"e_orders.amount_to_daily_totals.total","source":"orders","target":"daily_totals","sourceHandle":"amount","targetHandle":"total","animated":true,"style":{"stroke":"#64748b","strokeWidth":2},"label":"SUM()","labelStyle":{"fill":"#64748b","fontWeight":500,"fontSize":11},"labelBgStyle":{"fill":"#ffffff","fillOpacity":0.9}}]},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}# Or pipe from extract_lineage() extract_lineage("SELECT id, name FROM customers") |>   lineage_flow()  {"x":{"nodes":[{"id":"customers","type":"tableNode","data":{"label":"customers","columns":["id","name"],"tableType":"source","colors":{"bg":"#f0f7ff","border":"#3b82f6","header":"#1d4ed8"}},"position":{"x":0,"y":0},"draggable":true,"sourcePosition":"right","targetPosition":"left"},{"id":"output","type":"tableNode","data":{"label":"output","columns":["id","name"],"tableType":"target","colors":{"bg":"#f0fdf4","border":"#10b981","header":"#059669"}},"position":{"x":400,"y":0},"draggable":true,"sourcePosition":"right","targetPosition":"left"}],"edges":[{"id":"e_customers.id_to_output.id","source":"customers","target":"output","sourceHandle":"id","targetHandle":"id","animated":false,"style":{"stroke":"#64748b","strokeWidth":2}},{"id":"e_customers.name_to_output.name","source":"customers","target":"output","sourceHandle":"name","targetHandle":"name","animated":false,"style":{"stroke":"#64748b","strokeWidth":2}}]},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}

title: Export lineage as GraphML — lineage_graphml
text: Serializes lineage object GraphML, XML graph format read igraph, Gephi, yEd, graph tools. table column becomes node (id "table.column", duplicated name attribute igraph picks vertex name) table, column, node_type attributes; column-level edge becomes directed edge. granularity makes export useful downstream: igraph::subcomponent(g, "output.total", mode = "") lists every source column feeding output, Gephi can color graph table.

title: Export lineage as GraphML — lineage_graphml
code: lineage_graphml(lineage, path = NULL)

title: Export lineage as GraphML — lineage_graphml
text: lineage result extract_lineage(), list nodes edges built create_table_node() create_column_edge(). path Optional file write GraphML . supplied, string returned invisibly.

title: Export lineage as GraphML — lineage_graphml
text: string containing GraphML document.

title: Export lineage as GraphML — lineage_graphml
code: lineage <- list(   nodes = list(     create_table_node("orders", c("order_id", "amount")),     create_table_node("daily_totals", "total", table_type = "target")   ),   edges = list(     create_column_edge("orders", "amount", "daily_totals", "total")   ) ) cat(lineage_graphml(lineage)) #> <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> #> <graphml xmlns="http://graphml.graphdrawing.org/xmlns" #>          xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" #>          xsi:schemaLocation="http://graphml.graphdrawing.org/xmlns http://graphml.graphdrawing.org/xmlns/1.0/graphml.xsd"> #>   <key id="name" for="node" attr.name="name" attr.type="string"/> #>   <key id="table" for="node" attr.name="table" attr.type="string"/> #>   <key id="column" for="node" attr.name="column" attr.type="string"/> #>   <key id="node_type" for="node" attr.name="node_type" attr.type="string"/> #>   <graph id="lineage" edgedefault="directed"> #>     <node id="orders.order_id"> #>       <data key="name">orders.order_id</data> #>       <data key="table">orders</data> #>       <data key="column">order_id</data> #>       <data key="node_type">source</data> #>     </node> #>     <node id="orders.amount"> #>       <data key="name">orders.amount</data> #>       <data key="table">orders</data> #>       <data key="column">amount</data> #>       <data key="node_type">source</data> #>     </node> #>     <node id="daily_totals.total"> #>       <data key="name">daily_totals.total</data> #>       <data key="table">daily_totals</data> #>       <data key="column">total</data> #>       <data key="node_type">target</data> #>     </node> #>     <edge source="orders.amount" target="daily_totals.total"/> #>   </graph> #> </graphml>  # Round-trip through igraph for ancestry queries path <- tempfile(fileext = ".graphml") lineage_graphml(lineage, path = path) g <- igraph::read_graph(path, format = "graphml") igraph::subcomponent(g, "daily_totals.total", mode = "in") #> + 2/3 vertices, named, from 75b0cfb: #> [1] daily_totals.total orders.amount

title: Export lineage as JSON — lineage_json
text: Serializes lineage object small, stable JSON document: node ids columns table type, plus one record per column-level edge. React Flow presentation details (positions, colors) deliberately dropped, output suitable scripting jq, committing version control (CI diff catches accidental provenance changes pipeline edited), feeding data catalog.

title: Export lineage as JSON — lineage_json
code: lineage_json(lineage, path = NULL, pretty = TRUE)

title: Export lineage as JSON — lineage_json
text: lineage result extract_lineage(), list nodes edges built create_table_node() create_column_edge(). path Optional file write JSON . supplied, string returned invisibly. pretty TRUE (default), indent output readability. Use FALSE single-line document.

title: Export lineage as JSON — lineage_json
text: JSON string. metadata (present extract_lineage() results), nodes (objects id, type, columns), edges (objects source, source_column, target, target_column).

title: Export lineage as JSON — lineage_json
code: lineage <- list(   nodes = list(     create_table_node("orders", c("order_id", "amount")),     create_table_node("daily_totals", "total", table_type = "target")   ),   edges = list(     create_column_edge("orders", "amount", "daily_totals", "total")   ) ) lineage_json(lineage) #> { #>   "nodes": [ #>     { #>       "id": "orders", #>       "type": "source", #>       "columns": ["order_id", "amount"] #>     }, #>     { #>       "id": "daily_totals", #>       "type": "target", #>       "columns": ["total"] #>     } #>   ], #>   "edges": [ #>     { #>       "source": "orders", #>       "source_column": "amount", #>       "target": "daily_totals", #>       "target_column": "total" #>     } #>   ] #> }   # Write to a file instead path <- tempfile(fileext = ".json") lineage_json(lineage, path = path) extract_lineage("SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total                  FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id") |>   lineage_json() #> { #>   "metadata": { #>     "sql": "SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total\n                 FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id", #>     "dialect": "duckdb", #>     "engine": "sqlglot", #>     "table_count": 2, #>     "edge_count": 2 #>   }, #>   "nodes": [ #>     { #>       "id": "orders", #>       "type": "source", #>       "columns": ["customer_id", "amount"] #>     }, #>     { #>       "id": "output", #>       "type": "target", #>       "columns": ["customer_id", "total"] #>     } #>   ], #>   "edges": [ #>     { #>       "source": "orders", #>       "source_column": "customer_id", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "customer_id" #>     }, #>     { #>       "source": "orders", #>       "source_column": "amount", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "total" #>     } #>   ] #> }

title: dplyneage (development version)
text: New lineage_json() lineage_graphml() export extract_lineage() results (hand-built node/edge lists) interchange formats: clean JSON schema scripting, CI diffs, data catalogs, column-level GraphML loads directly igraph, Gephi, yEd impact analysis. extract_lineage() now analyzes dbplyr lazy tables pure-R engine walks pipeline’s lazy query tree — Python required. Column provenance read directly tree, joins (including suffix conflicts coalesced full-join keys), aggregates, window expressions, set operations resolve exactly. New engine argument extract_lineage(): "auto" (default) uses R engine lazy tables falls back sqlglot SQL strings constructs R engine trace, raw SQL injected dbplyr::sql(); "r" "sqlglot" force specific engine. Requires dbplyr >= 2.5.0 R engine. extract_lineage() results now record engine ran metadata$engine.

title: dplyneage 0.1.0
text: First public release.

title: dplyneage 0.1.0
text: extract_lineage() extracts column-level lineage dplyr/dbplyr pipelines raw SQL strings, powered sqlglot’s lineage engine. Aliases, CTEs, subqueries, set operations (UNION), multi-source computed columns (e.g. COALESCE(.x, b.x)) resolve true source columns. Schema-aware column attribution: given dbplyr lazy table, extract_lineage() automatically reads referenced table’s columns database connection unqualified columns attributed correct table SELECT * expands. raw SQL, pass new schema argument. lineage_flow() renders interactive React Flow diagrams column-level edges, draggable table nodes, hover highlighting, zoom/pan controls. Accepts extract_lineage() output directly pipe. create_table_node() create_column_edge() building lineage diagrams manually, plus lineage_example() built-demo. Shiny bindings via lineage_flowOutput() renderLineageFlow(). Multiple SQL dialects supported via sqlglot (DuckDB default; PostgreSQL, MySQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, ). Python dependencies provisioned automatically reticulate::py_require() — manual setup step. install_sqlglot() deprecated nothing.

title: dplyneage 0.1.0
text: Works box package produces dbplyr lazy tables, including ducklake. React Flow JavaScript bundle ships pre-built package.
